audit_status.source_base: Hebrew Numbers / Bemidbar Balaam blessing-oracle text tradition, draft aligned later
audit_status.review_note: First-pass draft reviewed for seven-altars ritual, first and second oracles, inability to curse the blessed, Israel's distinctness, poetic innocence language, Exodus strength, anti-divination claim, and lion imagery.

verses.restored: Balaam said to Balak, Build for me here seven altars and prepare for me here seven bulls and seven rams.
Balak did as Balaam spoke, and Balak and Balaam offered a bull and a ram on each altar.
Balaam said to Balak, Station yourself by your burnt offering, and I will go; perhaps YHWH will meet me. Whatever he shows me I will tell you. And he went to a bare height.
Divine met Balaam, and he said to him, I have arranged seven altars and offered a bull and a ram on each altar.
YHWH put a word in Balaam's mouth and said, Return to Balak, and thus you shall speak.
He returned to him, and look, he was standing by his burnt offering, he and all leaders of Moab.
He took up his oracle and said: From Aram Balak brought me, king of Moab from mountains of east: Come, curse Jacob for me; come, denounce Israel.
How shall I curse whom El has not cursed? How shall I denounce whom YHWH has not denounced?
For from top of rocks I see him, and from hills I behold him. Look, a people dwelling apart, and among nations it does not reckon itself.
Who has counted dust of Jacob or numbered fourth part of Israel? Let my soul/life die death of upright, and let my end be like his.
Balak said to Balaam, What have you done to me? To curse my enemies I took you, and look, you have surely blessed.
He answered and said, Must I not keep to speak what YHWH puts in my mouth?
Balak said to him, Please go with me to another place from which you may see him. Only his edge you shall see, and all of him you shall not see; curse him for me from there.
He took him to field of Zophim/Watchers, to top of Pisgah; he built seven altars and offered a bull and a ram on each altar.
Balaam said to Balak, Station yourself here by your burnt offering, while I meet YHWH yonder.
YHWH met Balaam, put a word in his mouth, and said, Return to Balak, and thus you shall speak.
He came to him, and look, he was standing by his burnt offering, and leaders of Moab with him. Balak said to him, What has YHWH spoken?
He took up his oracle and said: Rise, Balak, and hear; give ear to me, son of Zippor.
El is not a man that he should lie, nor a human son that he should change his mind. Has he said and will he not do? Has he spoken and will he not establish?
Look, to bless I have received; he has blessed, and I cannot turn it back.
He has not beheld iniquity in Jacob, nor seen trouble in Israel. YHWH his Divine is with him, and shout of a king is among him.
El brings them out from Egypt; like horns/strength of wild ox is he for him.
For there is no enchantment against Jacob and no divination against Israel. At the proper time it is said to Jacob and to Israel what El has done.
Look, a people rises like lioness and lifts itself like lion; it does not lie down until it eats prey and drinks blood of slain.
Balak said to Balaam, Neither curse it at all nor bless it at all.
Balaam answered and said to Balak, Did I not speak to you, saying, All that YHWH speaks, that I shall do?
Balak said to Balaam, Please come, I will take you to another place. Perhaps it will be right in eyes of Divine that you curse it for me from there.
Balak took Balaam to top of Peor, overlooking face of Jeshimon/wasteland.
Balaam said to Balak, Build for me here seven altars and prepare for me here seven bulls and seven rams.
Balak did as Balaam said, and offered a bull and a ram on each altar.

translation_notes: Balaam's oracles transform a commissioned curse into blessing; royal payment and repeated ritual cannot purchase a different word.
'Dwelling apart' is poetic description of distinct identity in this oracle, not a timeless command for ethnic isolation, segregation, or supremacy.
Verse 21's claim that no iniquity is seen is covenant-poetic language in context, not a claim that Israel or its leaders are morally flawless.
The text rejects enchantment and divination as effective against the blessed people; it is not a manual for counter-magic.
Wild-ox and lion imagery expresses ancient strength and victory rhetoric and must not authorize modern violence or dehumanization.
Balak repeatedly changes Balaam's viewpoint in hopes of controlling the outcome, while the blessing remains resistant to manipulation.
safety_note: Do not use Numbers 23 to promote ethnic supremacy, segregation, magical warfare, antisemitic curse traditions, or nationalist violence. The core movement is the failure of paid power to turn a people into the object of a curse.
